description: Victorian Curriculum (VCAA) — English and Science for Levels 7-10. Aligned to curriculum content descriptions.

exam.name: Victorian Curriculum
exam.subject: English
exam.description: English curriculum covering Language, Literature and Literacy strands for Levels 7-10.

topics.title: Language for Interaction
topics.concept_explanation: Understanding how language is used to evaluate texts and how evaluations can be substantiated by reference to the text and other sources.

examples.question: Identify the evaluative language in: 'The stunning performance captivated the audience, making it a truly remarkable evening.'
solution_steps: 'stunning' — positive adjective (evaluation)
'captivated' — strong verb showing effect
'truly remarkable' — intensifier + evaluative adjective
examples.answer: stunning, captivated, truly remarkable — all positive evaluative choices

examples.question: Rewrite in formal register: 'The movie was awesome and everyone loved it.'
solution_steps: Replace slang 'awesome' with formal equivalent
Replace vague 'everyone' with specific
Add evaluative precision
examples.answer: The film was exceptionally well-crafted and was widely acclaimed by audiences.

topics.title: Text Structure and Organisation
topics.concept_explanation: Understanding how text structures and language features become more complex in informative and persuasive texts. Identifying structures like taxonomies, cause and effect, and extended metaphors.
key_points: How authors guide readers through texts

examples.question: What is the purpose of a topic sentence in an informative paragraph?
solution_steps: A topic sentence introduces the main idea of the paragraph
It tells the reader what the paragraph will be about
Supporting sentences then provide evidence/detail
examples.answer: The topic sentence states the main idea, guiding the reader and providing structure for the paragraph.

examples.question: Identify the text structure: 'Due to deforestation, many species lost their habitats. As a result, biodiversity declined sharply.'
solution_steps: 'Due to' signals a cause
'As a result' signals an effect
This is cause and effect structure
examples.answer: Cause and effect — deforestation (cause) → habitat loss → biodiversity decline (effects)

topics.title: Expressing and Developing Ideas
topics.concept_explanation: Analysing how point of view is generated in visual texts through choices like gaze, angle and social distance. Understanding abstract nouns and specialised vocabulary.

examples.question: How does a low camera angle affect the viewer's perception of a character?
solution_steps: Low angle = camera looks up at the subject
This makes the subject appear larger/more powerful
Conveys authority, dominance, or importance
examples.answer: A low angle makes the character appear powerful, dominant, or important by forcing the viewer to look up at them.

topics.title: Responding to Literature
topics.concept_explanation: Comparing how language and images create character and influence emotions. Discussing aesthetic and social value of texts using metalanguage.

examples.question: What is the difference between a flat character and a round character?
solution_steps: Flat character: simple, one-dimensional, predictable
Round character: complex, multi-faceted, develops over time
Round characters are more realistic and engaging
examples.answer: A flat character has one or two traits and is predictable. A round character is complex, has depth, and changes throughout the story.

examples.question: How can a text have both aesthetic and social value?
solution_steps: Aesthetic value: beautiful language, imagery, structure
Social value: addresses issues, promotes understanding
A novel like 'To Kill a Mockingbird' has both: beautiful prose AND addresses racism
examples.answer: Aesthetic value comes from language craft and style; social value comes from addressing real-world issues. Many great texts combine both.

topics.title: Examining Literature
topics.concept_explanation: Recognising how characterisation, events and settings combine in narratives. Understanding how language is compressed in film/drama and creates layers of meaning in poetry.

examples.question: Identify the literary device: 'The wind whispered through the ancient trees.'
solution_steps: The wind is given a human action — 'whispered'
Non-human things given human qualities = personification
examples.answer: Personification — the wind is given the human ability to whisper.

examples.question: What effect does the metaphor 'Life is a journey' create?
solution_steps: Compares life to a journey without using 'like' or 'as'
Implies progress, direction, obstacles
Creates meaning about experiences and growth
examples.answer: It implies life has a direction, involves challenges and milestones, and suggests personal growth — making abstract experience concrete.

topics.title: Creating Literature
topics.concept_explanation: Using personal knowledge and literary texts as starting points to create imaginative writing in different forms and genres.

examples.question: Rewrite using 'show don't tell': 'Sarah was scared.'
solution_steps: Don't state the emotion directly
Show physical reactions and behaviours
Use sensory details
examples.answer: Sarah's hands trembled as she pressed her back against the cold wall, her breath coming in short, ragged gasps.

topics.title: Interpreting, Analysing, Evaluating
topics.concept_explanation: Using comprehension strategies to interpret, analyse and synthesise ideas from a variety of textual sources.

examples.question: What is the difference between an inference and a stated fact?
solution_steps: Stated fact: directly written in the text
Inference: conclusion drawn from evidence + reasoning
Inference requires 'reading between the lines'
examples.answer: A stated fact is explicitly written in the text. An inference is a logical conclusion you draw from clues in the text combined with your own knowledge.

topics.title: Creating Texts
topics.concept_explanation: Creating spoken, written and multimodal texts for different purposes and audiences. Editing for meaning and effect.

examples.question: What should you consider when writing for a teenage audience vs an adult audience?
solution_steps: Vocabulary: simpler/relatable vs more formal
Tone: casual/engaging vs authoritative
References: pop culture vs professional/historical
Length: shorter/punchy vs detailed
examples.answer: For teenagers: use relatable language, engaging tone, contemporary references. For adults: use more formal vocabulary, measured tone, and detailed evidence.

topics.title: Grammar and Punctuation
topics.concept_explanation: Understanding punctuation and grammar in complex sentences. Combining clauses, using prepositional phrases, and applying correct conventions.

examples.question: Combine using a subordinate clause: 'The students studied hard. They passed the exam.'
solution_steps: Identify the relationship: cause → effect
Use subordinating conjunction: 'because', 'since', 'as'
Combine: 'Because the students studied hard, they passed the exam.'
examples.answer: Because the students studied hard, they passed the exam.

examples.question: Convert to passive voice: 'The scientist discovered a new species.'
solution_steps: Active: subject (scientist) does the action
Passive: object becomes subject, verb becomes 'was + past participle'
'A new species was discovered by the scientist.'
examples.answer: A new species was discovered by the scientist.

topics.title: Oral Presentations and Discussions
topics.concept_explanation: Planning, rehearsing and delivering presentations. Using voice, body language and visual aids effectively.

examples.question: List three techniques for an effective oral presentation.
solution_steps: 1. Eye contact — connect with audience
2. Varied pace — slow for emphasis, faster for energy
3. Structured argument — clear introduction, evidence, conclusion
examples.answer: Eye contact, varied pace and tone, and a clear structure with evidence.

exam.name: Victorian Curriculum
exam.subject: Science
exam.description: Science curriculum covering Biological, Chemical, Earth & Space, and Physical sciences for Levels 7-10.

topics.title: Classification and Diversity
topics.concept_explanation: There are differences within and between groups of organisms. Classification helps organise this diversity using hierarchical systems.
key_points: Kingdom, Phylum, Class, Order, Family, Genus, Species

examples.question: Put these classification levels in order from broadest to most specific: Class, Species, Kingdom, Family, Genus, Order, Phylum
solution_steps: Kingdom (broadest)
Phylum
Class
Order
Family
Genus
Species (most specific)
Remember: King Philip Came Over For Good Spaghetti
examples.answer: Kingdom → Phylum → Class → Order → Family → Genus → Species

topics.title: Cells — Structure and Function
topics.concept_explanation: Cells are the basic units of living things. They have specialised structures (organelles) and functions. Plant and animal cells differ.
key_points: Plant cells: cell wall, chloroplasts, large vacuole

examples.question: Compare plant and animal cells — name 3 differences.
solution_steps: 1. Cell wall: plant cells have rigid cell wall, animal cells don't
2. Chloroplasts: plant cells have them (photosynthesis), animal cells don't
3. Vacuole: plant cells have one large central vacuole, animal cells have small ones or none
examples.answer: Plant cells have: cell wall, chloroplasts, large central vacuole. Animal cells lack these.

examples.question: Why are mitochondria called the 'powerhouse of the cell'?
solution_steps: Mitochondria carry out cellular respiration
Glucose + oxygen → carbon dioxide + water + energy (ATP)
They convert food energy into usable energy (ATP)
examples.answer: Mitochondria convert glucose into ATP (energy) through cellular respiration — they produce the cell's energy supply.

topics.title: Food Chains, Webs and Ecosystems
topics.concept_explanation: Interactions between organisms can be described as food chains and food webs. Human activity can affect these interactions.
key_points: Producer → primary consumer → secondary consumer → tertiary consumer
Only ~10% of energy transfers to next level
Human impacts: deforestation, pollution, introduced species

examples.question: In a food web, what would happen if all the frogs were removed?
solution_steps: Frogs eat insects → insect population would increase
Frogs are eaten by snakes → snake population would decrease
Increased insects could damage crops/plants
This shows interconnectedness of food webs
examples.answer: Insect populations would increase (no predator), snake populations would decrease (less food), and there would be cascading effects throughout the ecosystem.

topics.title: Body Systems
topics.concept_explanation: Multicellular organisms contain systems of organs that carry out specialised functions for survival and reproduction.

examples.question: Trace the path of food through the digestive system.
solution_steps: Mouth: mechanical + chemical digestion (teeth + saliva/amylase)
Oesophagus: peristalsis moves food to stomach
Stomach: acid + pepsin breaks down protein
Small intestine: most absorption occurs (villi)
Large intestine: absorbs water
Rectum → anus: waste eliminated
examples.answer: Mouth → oesophagus → stomach → small intestine (absorption) → large intestine (water) → rectum → anus

topics.title: Mixtures and Separation
topics.concept_explanation: Mixtures contain combinations of pure substances that can be separated using physical techniques based on different properties.
key_points: Based on physical properties (boiling point, solubility, particle size)

examples.question: How would you separate sand from salt water?
solution_steps: Step 1: Filtration — sand is insoluble, trapped by filter paper
Step 2: Evaporation — heat the salt water
Step 3: Salt crystals remain as water evaporates
examples.answer: Filtration to remove sand, then evaporation to recover salt from the solution.

topics.title: Particle Model of Matter
topics.concept_explanation: The properties of solids, liquids and gases can be explained by the motion and arrangement of particles.
key_points: Solids: particles close, vibrate, fixed shape
Liquids: particles close, slide past each other, takes shape of container
Gases: particles far apart, move freely, fill container

examples.question: Using the particle model, explain why gases are compressible but liquids are not.
solution_steps: Gas particles are far apart with lots of empty space
When compressed, particles move closer together into the empty space
Liquid particles are already close together
No empty space to compress into
examples.answer: Gas particles have large spaces between them that can be compressed. Liquid particles are already closely packed with minimal space, so they resist compression.

topics.title: Elements, Compounds and Chemical Reactions
topics.concept_explanation: Elements combine to form compounds. Chemical reactions involve rearranging atoms to form new substances with different properties.
key_points: Compound: two or more elements chemically bonded
Chemical reaction signs: colour change, gas produced, temperature change
Conservation of mass: atoms rearranged, not created/destroyed

examples.question: What is the difference between an element, a compound and a mixture?
solution_steps: Element: made of only one type of atom (e.g., O₂)
Compound: two or more elements chemically bonded (e.g., H₂O)
Mixture: substances physically combined, not bonded (e.g., salt water)
examples.answer: Element = one atom type. Compound = different atoms chemically bonded. Mixture = substances physically combined (can be separated).

topics.title: Earth's Resources and Sustainability
topics.concept_explanation: Some of Earth's resources are renewable and some are non-renewable. Human activity can affect the availability and quality of resources.
key_points: Sustainability: using resources without depleting them
Human impact: climate change, pollution, deforestation

examples.question: Why are fossil fuels considered non-renewable?
solution_steps: Fossil fuels formed over millions of years from dead organisms
We use them much faster than they form
Once depleted, they cannot be replaced in human timescales
examples.answer: Fossil fuels take millions of years to form but are consumed rapidly. They cannot be replenished within human lifetimes, making them non-renewable.

topics.title: Rock Cycle and Geological Change
topics.concept_explanation: Sedimentary, igneous and metamorphic rocks contain minerals and are formed by processes operating at different timescales.
key_points: Sedimentary: from compressed layers of sediment
Metamorphic: changed by heat and pressure
Rock cycle: continuous transformation between types

examples.question: How does sedimentary rock form?
solution_steps: Weathering breaks down existing rocks into sediments
Erosion transports sediments to rivers, lakes, seas
Layers of sediment build up over time
Compaction and cementation turn sediment into rock
examples.answer: Weathering → erosion → deposition → compaction and cementation over millions of years.

topics.title: Forces and Motion
topics.concept_explanation: Change to an object's motion is caused by unbalanced forces acting on it. Earth's gravity pulls objects towards the centre of the Earth.
key_points: Balanced forces: no change in motion

examples.question: A book sits on a table. What forces act on it and are they balanced?
solution_steps: Weight (gravity): pulls book downward
Normal force: table pushes book upward
These forces are equal and opposite
Net force = 0 → balanced → book stays still
examples.answer: Weight (down) and normal force (up) are balanced — the book remains stationary.

topics.title: Energy Transfers and Transformations
topics.concept_explanation: Energy can be transferred and transformed. It is conserved in a closed system. Common forms: kinetic, potential, thermal, electrical, light, sound.

examples.question: A ball is thrown upward. Describe the energy transformations.
solution_steps: At launch: maximum kinetic energy, minimum PE
Rising: KE converts to gravitational PE
At peak: KE = 0, maximum PE
Falling: PE converts back to KE
Energy is conserved throughout (ignoring air resistance)
examples.answer: KE → PE (going up) → KE (coming down). Total energy stays constant — it transforms between kinetic and potential.

topics.title: Light and Sound Waves
topics.concept_explanation: Light can form images using the properties of reflection and refraction. Sound is produced by vibrations and travels as longitudinal waves.
key_points: Light: transverse wave, doesn't need medium
Reflection: angle of incidence = angle of reflection
Refraction: light bends when changing medium

examples.question: Why can't sound travel through space but light can?
solution_steps: Sound is a mechanical wave — needs particles to vibrate
Space is a vacuum — no particles
Light is an electromagnetic wave — doesn't need a medium
Light can travel through vacuum
examples.answer: Sound needs a medium (particles) to travel, and space is a vacuum. Light is an electromagnetic wave that can travel through empty space.
